title: Event System — Publishing & Listening
description: Master the SAP Commerce Cloud event framework for decoupled, asynchronous communication between modules.

sections.title: Why Events Matter in Commerce Cloud
sections.content:
SAP Commerce Cloud uses an event-driven architecture to let extensions communicate without creating hard dependencies on each other. Instead of one service calling another directly, a service publishes an event, and any interested component listens for it.

This pattern delivers three key benefits. First, loose coupling: the publisher doesn't need to know who (or how many) listeners exist. Second, extensibility: custom extensions can hook into platform behaviour simply by registering a listener — no core code changes required. Third, scalability: events can be processed synchronously on the same thread or asynchronously on a different thread or even a different cluster node, depending on configuration.

The platform ships with dozens of built-in events — order placement, cart calculation, login, cache invalidation, and more. Understanding the event lifecycle is essential for writing clean, maintainable customisations that respect upgrade safety.

sections.title: Core Concepts — Events, Sources & the EventService
sections.content:
Every event in Commerce Cloud is a Java object that extends AbstractEvent. This base class carries metadata such as a timestamp, the source object, and the cluster node ID. For events that must travel across cluster nodes, you extend ClusterAwareEvent instead.

The central API is EventService, which exposes two main methods: publishEvent(AbstractEvent event) to fire an event, and registerEventListener(EventListener listener) to subscribe. In practice you rarely call registerEventListener manually; Spring wiring handles it.

When publishEvent is called the DefaultEventService iterates over all registered listeners whose getHandledEventTypes() includes the published event class. Listeners are invoked in no guaranteed order. If the event is a ClusterAwareEvent, the service also serialises it and broadcasts it to other cluster nodes via the cluster broadcast mechanism, so remote listeners receive it too.

You can create your own event simply by subclassing AbstractEvent (or ClusterAwareEvent) and adding whatever payload fields you need — for example an order code, a customer UID, or a list of modified product codes.

sections.title: Creating Custom Events
sections.content:
A custom event is a plain Java class. At minimum it extends AbstractEvent and adds fields for the data listeners will need.

public class InventoryThresholdEvent extends AbstractEvent {
    private final String productCode;
    private final int availableStock;
    private final int threshold;

    public InventoryThresholdEvent(final String productCode,
                                    final int availableStock,
                                    final int threshold) {
        super();
        this.productCode = productCode;
        this.availableStock = availableStock;
        this.threshold = threshold;
    }
    // getters
}

If the event must be received by listeners on every cluster node — for example a cache-flush signal — extend ClusterAwareEvent instead and implement publish(int sourceNodeId, int targetNodeId). The platform serialises cluster-aware events, so all payload fields must be Serializable.

Best practices: keep events immutable (use final fields, no setters), carry only the minimum data required (codes and IDs rather than full model objects), and name them with a clear past-tense or threshold convention (e.g. OrderPlacedEvent, StockLevelLowEvent).

sections.title: Implementing Event Listeners
sections.content:
A listener implements EventListener<T extends AbstractEvent> or extends the convenience base class AbstractEventListener<T>. The generic parameter T tells the framework which event type this listener handles.

public class InventoryThresholdListener
        extends AbstractEventListener<InventoryThresholdEvent> {

    @Override
    protected void onEvent(final InventoryThresholdEvent event) {
        // e.g. send alert email, create a restock task
        LOG.warn("Stock low for {} : {} remaining",
                 event.getProductCode(),
                 event.getAvailableStock());
    }
}

Register the listener as a Spring bean in your *-spring.xml. The platform auto-detects beans that implement EventListener and registers them with the EventService at startup.

<bean id="inventoryThresholdListener"
      class="com.acme.listeners.InventoryThresholdListener"
      parent="abstractEventListener" />

Setting the parent to abstractEventListener is important — it wires tenant-awareness and ensures the listener is properly initialised. Forgetting this parent is a common source of bugs where listeners silently fail in multi-tenant setups.

A single listener class should handle one event type. If you need to react to multiple events, create separate listener beans rather than building a monolithic switch statement.

sections.title: Synchronous vs Asynchronous Processing
sections.content:
By default, events are processed synchronously — the publishing thread blocks until every listener has finished. This is fine for fast, lightweight reactions (e.g. setting a flag), but problematic for heavy work like sending emails or calling external APIs.

To process an event asynchronously, override the listener method isAsynchronous() (from AbstractEventListener) to return true, or configure an asynchronous event processing strategy via Spring. The platform then hands off execution to a thread pool, freeing the publisher immediately.

Key considerations for asynchronous listeners: transaction context — the listener runs outside the publisher's transaction, so the database changes that triggered the event may not yet be committed. Use TransactionAwareEvent patterns or the afterCommit hook to avoid reading stale data. Error handling — exceptions in async listeners are logged but do not roll back the publisher's transaction. Ordering — execution order across multiple async listeners is non-deterministic.

A useful rule of thumb: if the listener's work must succeed for the business operation to be valid (e.g. stock reservation), keep it synchronous. If it is supplementary (e.g. analytics, notification), make it asynchronous.

sections.title: Platform Events, Cluster Broadcasting & Testing
sections.content:
Commerce Cloud fires many events out of the box. Key examples include AfterItemCreationEvent, AfterItemRemovalEvent, OrderPlacedEvent, LoginSuccessEvent, BeforeSessionCloseEvent, and the AfterInitializationEndEvent that signals the system is fully started. Hooking into these lets you extend platform behaviour without modifying core source.

Cluster broadcasting works through ClusterAwareEvent. When such an event is published, the platform's ClusterBroadcastService serialises the event and sends it to every node. Each node's EventService then dispatches it to local listeners. This is how cache invalidation is synchronised across a CCV2 cluster.

Testing event-driven code in isolation is straightforward. In unit tests, instantiate your listener and call onEvent() directly. For integration tests, publish an event through the EventService retrieved from the Spring context and assert the expected side effects (database changes, log output, mock invocations). You can also write a test listener that captures events into a list to verify publishing logic.

Common pitfalls to avoid: circular event chains (Listener A publishes event B, whose listener publishes event A), performing heavy blocking I/O in synchronous listeners, and forgetting Serializable on ClusterAwareEvent payload fields — which causes silent broadcast failures.

questions.text: What base class must all custom events extend at minimum in SAP Commerce Cloud?
questions.explanation: All events in Commerce Cloud extend AbstractEvent. ClusterAwareEvent is a subclass of AbstractEvent used only when the event must be broadcast across cluster nodes.

questions.text: Which service is responsible for publishing and dispatching events to listeners?
questions.explanation: EventService is the central API. Its publishEvent() method fires the event and dispatches it to all matching registered listeners.

questions.text: Event listeners registered with the EventService are guaranteed to execute in the order they were registered.
questions.explanation: The platform does not guarantee execution order of listeners. If ordering matters, you must implement coordination logic yourself or combine the work into a single listener.

questions.text: What is the recommended parent bean when declaring a custom event listener in your Spring XML configuration?
questions.explanation: Setting parent="abstractEventListener" ensures the listener is properly wired with tenant-awareness and initialisation hooks. Omitting this parent can cause silent failures in multi-tenant environments.

questions.text: ClusterAwareEvent payloads must implement the Serializable interface so the platform can broadcast them to other nodes.
questions.explanation: Because ClusterAwareEvent instances are serialised and sent across the network to other cluster nodes, all payload fields must be Serializable. Non-serializable fields will cause silent broadcast failures.

questions.text: By default, how are events processed when published through the EventService?
options: Asynchronously on a dedicated thread pool
Queued in a JMS broker for later processing
Batched and processed at the end of the transaction
questions.explanation: By default, event processing is synchronous: the publishing thread blocks until all listeners have completed. Asynchronous processing requires explicit opt-in via listener configuration.

questions.text: An exception thrown by an asynchronous event listener will roll back the publishing service's database transaction.
questions.explanation: Asynchronous listeners execute outside the publisher's transaction context. Exceptions in async listeners are logged but have no effect on the original transaction.

questions.text: Which of the following is a built-in platform event fired after the system has fully started?
questions.explanation: AfterInitializationEndEvent is the platform event that signals the system has completed its initialisation sequence and is fully operational.

questions.text: The recommended approach for handling multiple unrelated event types is to create one listener class with a switch statement that inspects the event type.
questions.explanation: Best practice is to create separate listener beans, each handling a single event type. Monolithic switch-based listeners violate the single-responsibility principle and are harder to maintain and test.

questions.text: When should you extend ClusterAwareEvent instead of AbstractEvent?
options: When the event carries more than three payload fields
When the event must be received by listeners on every cluster node
When the event is published inside a CronJob
questions.explanation: ClusterAwareEvent should be used when the event must be broadcast to all cluster nodes, such as cache invalidation signals. The number of fields, async processing, or CronJob context are not relevant factors.

questions.text: You can unit-test an event listener by directly instantiating it and calling the onEvent() method with a test event object.
questions.explanation: Since listeners are plain Java objects, you can instantiate them in a test, mock any dependencies, and call onEvent() directly. This is the recommended approach for unit testing event-driven logic.

questions.text: What is a key risk when a synchronous listener performs a long-running external API call?
options: The event is automatically converted to asynchronous
The publishing thread blocks, degrading response time for the original operation
The listener is deregistered by the platform after a timeout
The external API call is silently dropped
questions.explanation: In synchronous mode, the publishing thread waits for all listeners to complete. A slow listener (e.g. calling an external API) blocks that thread, directly impacting the performance of the business operation that published the event.

questions.text: Custom event classes should include setter methods so that listeners can modify event data after publication.
questions.explanation: Events should be immutable. Use final fields with only getters and no setters. Allowing listeners to mutate events can introduce subtle bugs, especially when multiple listeners process the same event instance.
